source.zh: 伊法口传经文（奥杜·伊法）；塞缪尔·约翰逊《约鲁巴人史》（1921）；E·博拉吉·伊多武《奥罗杜马雷：约鲁巴信仰中的神》（1962）；威廉·巴斯科姆《伊法占卜：西非神人之间的沟通》（1969）、《十六贝壳占卜》（1980）；万德·阿比姆博拉《伊法：伊法文学经文导论》（1976）；乌利·拜尔《约鲁巴神话》（1980）
source.en: The oral Ifa corpus (Odu Ifa); Samuel Johnson, The History of the Yorubas (1921); E. Bolaji Idowu, Olodumare: God in Yoruba Belief (1962); William Bascom, Ifa Divination: Communication Between Gods and Men in West Africa (1969) and Sixteen Cowries (1980); Wande Abimbola, Ifa: An Exposition of Ifa Literary Corpus (1976); Ulli Beier, Yoruba Myths (1980)

note.zh:
约鲁巴宗教是活着的口传信仰，没有创世之书。伊法经文共二百五十六个「奥杜」，由祭司巴巴拉沃逐句背诵，创世的材料散在各篇诗句里，从来没有被编成一部连贯的开端叙事；各城、各祭司团背的版本互不相同。今天能读到的文字版都出自十九世纪末以后：塞缪尔·约翰逊是奥约的圣公会牧师，1897年写完《约鲁巴人史》寄往伦敦，出版商把稿子弄丢了，他去世后由弟弟奥巴代亚凭笔记重建，1921年才印出来；他站在奥约的立场上，把奥杜杜瓦写成从东方率众迁来的凡人王子，不是神。本页最常被转述的那套细节（蜗牛壳装土、变色龙试地、「大地宽展了」），在伊多武1962年的记述里最为完整，后来的普及性转述多以他为本；而伊多武是基督教神学家，写这本书是为了论证约鲁巴信仰本质上是一神教，后来的学者指出他笔下的奥罗杜马雷被有意塑造得接近基督教的上帝——用他的材料，就得连这层滤镜一起看。

造陆的到底是谁，约鲁巴人自己没有定论，而且分歧的走向跟谁在说话有关。伊费的王统由奥杜杜瓦一系承袭，王室的讲法里就是奥杜杜瓦下的链子、倒的土；奥巴塔拉的祭司团坚持受命的本是奥巴塔拉，只是被棕榈酒误了事——也有讲法里他根本没醉，只是晚到一步。另有版本中奥罗杜马雷从一开始就直接派了奥杜杜瓦，奥巴塔拉不在场。奥杜杜瓦的性别也不固定：部分记录把他写成女性，与奥巴塔拉配为夫妇，属大地一路的神。器物细节同样有出入——链子有说金的有说铁的，鸟有说鸽子加五趾母鸡、有说只有一只母鸡。本页取的是伊费流传最广的合成版本，正文里把两说都保留了。棕榈酒因此在正文里出现两次——一次误了造陆，一次捏坏了人形——这是并置不同讲法的结果，并非同一则故事里的两场醉酒。

约鲁巴创世叙事里没有天地分离：天与水从一开始就是两层，谁也不需要把谁劈开、撑开。也没有神谱——故事开场时奥里沙已经在天上，没人讲他们从哪儿来，「最初的存在」这一格因此空着。同样没有灭世洪水，没有末日；「洪水与劫」这一格填的是奥巴塔拉与奥杜杜瓦的名分之争，这桩争执在伊费对应的是两个祭司团的长期对峙，与别处的洪水、末日不属同类事件，横向对照时请留意。伊费得名于「ilẹ̀ ń fẹ̀」（大地宽展了）是传统内部的说法，不是语言学的构拟。伊法与奥里沙崇拜今天仍在尼日利亚、贝宁及古巴桑特里亚、巴西坎东布莱中实行，各地讲法有别；巴巴拉沃掌握的许多经文只在入门传承中口授，未曾出版，本页只用已刊布的材料。
note.en:
Yoruba religion is a living oral tradition with no book of creation. The Ifa corpus consists of 256 odu, memorized verse by verse by babalawo priests; the creation material lies scattered through those verses and was never assembled into one connected account, and different towns and priesthoods carry different versions. Every written text available today dates from the late nineteenth century or after. Samuel Johnson, an Anglican pastor at Oyo, finished his History of the Yorubas in 1897 and sent it to London, where the publisher lost the manuscript; his brother Obadiah rebuilt it from the notes after his death and it appeared only in 1921. Johnson writes from Oyo's standpoint and makes Oduduwa a human prince who migrated from the east, not a god. The details most often retold — the snail shell of earth, the chameleon testing the ground, 'the earth spreads wide' — are set out most fully in Idowu (1962), the version popular retellings usually follow; Idowu was a Christian theologian whose purpose was to argue that Yoruba religion is essentially monotheistic, and later scholars have pointed out that his Olodumare is shaped to resemble the Christian God. Anyone using his material inherits that filter.

Who actually made the land is unsettled, and how a version comes out depends on who is telling it. Kingship at Ife descends through Oduduwa, and the royal tradition has him go down the chain and pour the earth. Obatala's priesthood holds that Obatala was the one commissioned and that palm wine cost him the work — in some tellings he is not drunk at all, merely late. Other versions have Olodumare send Oduduwa from the start, with no Obatala in the story. Oduduwa's gender is not fixed either: some records make Oduduwa female, consort of Obatala and a deity of the earth. The equipment varies too — a chain of gold or of iron, a pigeon with a five-toed hen or a hen alone. The sequence below follows the composite Ife version that is most widely told, and keeps both claimants in the text. Palm wine therefore turns up twice below — once costing Obatala the making of the land, once spoiling a batch of human bodies — which is what comes of setting different tellings side by side, not two drinking bouts inside a single story.

There is no separation of sky and earth in this cosmogony: the sky and the waters are two realms from the outset and nothing has to be split or propped apart. There is no theogony either — the orishas are already in the sky when the story opens, and nobody says where they came from, so the 'first beings' stage is left empty. Nor is there a world-destroying flood or an end of days. The 'ordeal' stage here is filled by the dispute between Obatala and Oduduwa, which at Ife corresponds to a long standoff between two priesthoods; readers comparing it with the floods and doomsdays of other traditions should keep that difference in view. Deriving Ile-Ife from 'ile n fe', 'the earth spreads wide', is a traditional etymology, not a linguistic reconstruction. Ifa and orisha worship are practiced today in Nigeria and Benin and in Cuban Santeria and Brazilian Candomble, each with its own tellings; much of what babalawo know is transmitted only through initiation and has never been published, and only published material is used here.

text.zh: 起初只有两层。上面是奥罗杜马雷的天域「奥伦」（ọ̀run），众奥里沙住在那里；下面是无边的水与沼泽，归海之主奥洛昆管辖，没有一寸站得住脚的干地。有的讲法说，奥里沙偶尔顺着蛛丝垂到下界，在泥水上打猎，猎完再爬回天上。下面没有人烟，没有田，也没有可以落脚建城的地方。
text.en: In the beginning there were two realms and no ground between them. Above lay orun, the sky, where Olodumare and the orishas lived. Below lay water and marsh, the domain of Olokun, owner of the sea, without a foot of dry land anywhere in it. Some tellings have the orishas letting themselves down on threads of spider silk to hunt over the swamp and climbing back up afterwards. Nothing lived down there, nothing could be planted, and there was nowhere to set a town.

title.zh: 一壳土、一只鸽子、一只五趾母鸡
title.en: A snail shell of earth, a pigeon, a five-toed hen

text.zh: 约鲁巴的口传没有神谱：故事开场时奥里沙已经在天上了，谁也不说他们从哪儿来。奥罗杜马雷把造陆的差事交给奥巴塔拉，给他一只装满松土的蜗牛壳、一只鸽子、一只五趾母鸡，还有一条从天垂到水面的链子。伊法之神奥伦米拉替这趟下行卜了一卦，随行做顾问——他被称作「命运的见证者」，创世时在场。造一个世界的全部家当，就是一壳土和两只鸟。
text.en: Yoruba tradition has no theogony: when the story opens the orishas are already in the sky, and nobody says where they came from. Olodumare gave the making of land to Obatala and handed him his equipment — a snail shell packed with loose earth, a pigeon, a hen with five toes, and a chain let down from the sky to the water. Orunmila, god of Ifa, cast the oracle for the descent and went along as adviser; he is called the witness to destiny, and he was there when the world was made. That was the whole inventory of creation: one shell of dirt and two birds.

title.en: The hen scratches the earth wide

text.zh: 奥巴塔拉半路上碰见棕榈酒，喝到醉倒睡去。奥杜杜瓦拿起那只蜗牛壳，顺链子下到水上，把土倒了下去；鸽子和五趾母鸡跳上土堆，一路刨，土越刨越散、越铺越远，成了旱地。落脚的那一点就是伊莱-伊费。奥罗杜马雷派变色龙下来验收——这东西走一步试一步，正合试地之用：第一趟回报地已够宽但还太软，第二趟回报又宽又实。伊费的口传说，「大地宽展了」（ilẹ̀ ń fẹ̀）这句话就是城名的来处。
text.en: Obatala met palm wine on the road and drank until he fell asleep. Oduduwa picked up the snail shell, went down the chain to the water and poured the earth out; the pigeon and the five-toed hen jumped onto the heap and scratched, and the soil they threw about spread until it was dry land. The spot where it began is Ile-Ife. Olodumare sent the chameleon down to inspect the work — a creature that tests each step before it puts its weight on it, which is what the job called for. It came back the first time reporting the ground was wide but still soft, and the second time that it was wide and firm. In the Ife telling, the words ile n fe, 'the earth spreads wide', are where the city's name comes from.

title.zh: 奥巴塔拉捏泥，奥罗杜马雷吹气

text.zh: 捏人的活儿归奥巴塔拉。他用黏土塑出人的躯体，一具具排开，奥罗杜马雷再吹进「埃米」（èmí，气息），泥胎才活过来。有一回他一边捏一边喝棕榈酒，手上没了准头，捏出驼背的、跛足的、白化的和瞎眼的人。酒醒之后他立誓终生不再碰棕榈酒，并把这些人认作自己的「埃尼奥里沙」——神的人，一世归他护佑。他的祭司至今守同一条禁忌，一身白布白珠。
text.en: The shaping of human bodies fell to Obatala. He molded them out of clay and laid them out in rows, and Olodumare breathed emi, breath, into them, and they lived. One day he drank palm wine while he worked and his hands went wrong: hunchbacks, the lame, albinos and the blind came out of that batch. Sober again, he swore never to touch palm wine, and took the people he had misshapen as eni orisha, people of the god, under his protection for life. His priests keep the same prohibition to this day and dress in white cloth and white beads.

title.zh: 去阿贾拉的作坊挑一个头
title.en: Picking a head in Ajala's workshop

text.zh: 身子是奥巴塔拉的手艺，命要各人自己去领。人在下生之前先上天，到陶匠阿贾拉的作坊里挑一个「奥里」（orí，内在的头）；阿贾拉手艺不匀，有烧透的，也有偷工的，挑到哪一个全看运气。选完再到奥罗杜马雷面前跪下，说出这一世要什么，得允之后才降生——而落地的那一刻，说过的话全忘光。所以约鲁巴人遇事就去请伊法：占卜是把当初跪着许下的那份命，再读一遍给本人听。
text.en: The body was Obatala's craft; the destiny each person had to go and fetch. Before birth a soul goes up to the workshop of Ajala, the potter who makes ori, the inner head. Ajala's work is uneven — some heads are properly fired, some are shoddy — and a soul takes its chances in the choosing. It then kneels before Olodumare and asks for the life it wants, and is granted it, and forgets every word of it in the moment of being born. This is why the Yoruba consult Ifa: the divination reads a person's own destiny back to him, the one spoken on his knees, which Orunmila stood by and witnessed.

text.zh: 造陆的功劳归谁，伊费自己也没结案。奥巴塔拉一派说受命的本是他，只是被棕榈酒误了；奥杜杜瓦一派说地是他倒的，链子是他下的，从头到尾就是他。两位神的祭司团在伊费长期对峙：王权归了奥杜杜瓦的血脉，奥巴塔拉的信众留在同一座城里，另有自己的祭典与游行，年年的仪式中至今留着两派对峙的痕迹。约鲁巴的创世叙事里没有灭世洪水，也没有诸神的末日，最接近一场大劫的，就是这桩争执。
text.en: Who made the land is a case Ife never closed. Obatala's side holds that he was the one commissioned and that palm wine cost him the work. Oduduwa's side holds that he went down the chain and poured out the earth, and that the task was his from the beginning. The two priesthoods have long stood opposed at Ife: kingship went to Oduduwa's line, while Obatala's people stayed in the same city with festivals and processions of their own, and the annual rites still carry the marks of that rivalry. Yoruba tradition has no world-drowning flood and no twilight of the gods. The nearest thing to a catastrophe in its account of creation is this dispute over whose work the world is.

title.en: The crowns go out from Ife

text.zh: 地造成之后，奥杜杜瓦就在伊费称王，是第一位圣王。传说他的子孙戴着串珠垂帘的王冠散往各处立国，奥约是其中之一；直到今天，只有能把世系接回奥杜杜瓦的君主才够格戴那种垂珠冠，伊费的「奥尼」被奉为他的直系继承人。伊费是大地起头的那一点，相传城中供奉四百零一位神灵；每年奥洛乔节，奥尼戴上传国的阿雷冠公开露面，祭奥贡，纪念开天辟地那一日。人死后归入祖先之列，再投生回自家门中——孩子取名「巴巴通德」（父亲回来了）、「耶通德」（母亲回来了），说的就是这件事。
text.en: With the land made, Oduduwa stayed on it and ruled as the first sacred king of Ife. Tradition says his children and grandchildren went out wearing beaded crowns and founded kingdoms, Oyo among them; to this day only a ruler who can trace his line back to Oduduwa may wear the fringed crown, and the Ooni of Ife reigns as his direct heir. Ife is the point at which the ground began, and is said to hold shrines to 401 divinities. Each year at the Olojo festival the Ooni appears in the ancestral Are crown to honor Ogun and to mark the day the world was made. The dead join the ranks of the ancestors and are born again into their own households, which is why Yoruba children are named Babatunde, 'father has come back', and Yetunde, 'mother has come back'.
